title: How many voters can a municipality hold? — Brazil
meta_desc: 739 Brazilian municipalities have more registered voters (TSE) than estimated residents (IBGE). An open dashboard placing the official data side by side, municipality by municipality.
h1:
How many voters can a
municipality hold?
geo_nota: Your location is used only in your browser — we never send it anywhere.
disclaimer: Important. A high ratio — even above 100% — is common and overwhelmingly legitimate: a voter's electoral domicile is not the same as their home address, people move without transferring their registration, and the IBGE population is an estimate. This is not a fraud detector: it simply places the official figures side by side, with the explanations.
lede: {n} municipalities have more registered voters (TSE) than estimated residents (IBGE). Explore the official comparison, municipality by municipality.
anos_ref: The “current” ratio = TSE electorate (generated {ger}) ÷ IBGE {ano} population estimate — the most recent of each, about a year apart. The most rigorous figure is the 2022 ratio (Census-based), shown in each city's profile.
tweet_hero: {n} Brazilian municipalities have more registered voters (TSE) than estimated residents (IBGE) — and it is almost always legitimate. See the official comparison, municipality by municipality:
entenda_c1_p: We divide the number of registered voters in the municipality (TSE) by the estimated population (IBGE). A ratio of 120% means 120 registered voters for every 100 estimated residents.
entenda_c2_h: Why does it go above 100%? it's normal
entenda_c2_p: Registration stays where a person signed up, not where they live now. Someone who moved without transferring still counts there. And the IBGE population is an estimate. That is why 739 municipalities have more voters than residents — almost always legitimately.
entenda_c3_p: Here the denominator counts only those old enough to vote (16+), about 80% of the population. Because the divisor is smaller, this figure is almost always higher: 64% of municipalities exceed 100% for that reason alone. Use the total ratio as your main reference.
entenda_c4_p: It is not a fraud detector. It is a starting point for investigation — always alongside the official explanations. A high ratio, on its own, does not indicate any irregularity.
map_dica_default: Zoom (scroll) or click a state to see its municipalities.
map_dica_zoom: Zoom in further to see city names.
foto_sub: Voters ÷ estimated population, spread across the — municipalities. Each bar is a ratio band.
hist_leg_le: {n} cities up to 100% of the population
rank_t_razao: The 15 municipalities with the highest voters/population ratio. The line marks 100%.
rank_t_saldo: Largest net voter inflow through registration transfers in {ano} (inflows − outflows).
rank_t_idoso: Largest share of the electorate aged 70 or older (optional voting).
rank_t_jovem: Largest share of voters aged 16–17 (optional voting).
rank_t_escol: Largest share of the electorate with primary education or less.
rank_t_facult: Largest share of optional voting (ages 16–17 and 70+).
rank_t_abst: Highest abstention in the most recent available election (2024 municipal, or 2022 general).
rank_t_margem: Municipalities where the net voter inflow in 2024 exceeded the mayor's margin of victory. This is a FACT (a comparison of two numbers) — it does not say how those voters voted, nor that they decided the election.
rank_t_rev3: Municipalities meeting the THREE cumulative criteria of TSE Resolution 23,659/2021 (art. 105) — the legally defined set. Meeting them is NOT an irregularity nor a review: any review is at the TSE's discretion.
rank_t_gasto: Highest DECLARED campaign spending per voter in {ano} (contracted expenses ÷ voters). These are figures declared to the TSE — they do not indicate irregularity or vote-buying; the ballot is secret. Normalized per voter so it isn't merely a ranking of large cities.
cmp_sub: Put two municipalities side by side — handy to settle that argument.
cmp_delta_nota: Δ is the A − B difference. Green when A is higher; red when B is higher. “—” = no data to compare.
chip_entrada_t: Municipalities where the 2024 net voter inflow exceeded the mayor's margin of victory. It is a fact — it does not say how they voted nor that they decided the election.
chip_rev3_t: Meets the THREE cumulative criteria of TSE Resolution 23,659/2021 (art. 105). Not an irregularity nor a review — any review is at the TSE's discretion.
chip_turno2_t: Municipalities whose 2024 mayoral election was decided in a runoff (over 200,000 voters).
chip_cem_t: Municipalities with more registered voters than estimated residents.
chip_tse_t: Electorate > 80% of the population — only the 3rd of the three cumulative criteria in TSE Resolution 23,659/2021. On its own it does not trigger a review.
chip_atip_t: “Atypical” = the ratio departs well from the national pattern (above Q3 + 1.5×IQR, Tukey's statistical rule). It is rare, not an irregularity.
th_razao_t: Voters ÷ estimated population, in %. 100% = as many voters as residents.
th_razao16_t: Voters ÷ estimated voting-age population (16+) — the 2022 Census share applied to the year's estimate. Easily exceeds 100% (smaller divisor); read it together with the total ratio.
expl_dica: Tap any municipality to open the full profile (transfers, abstention, demographics, the 2024 election and more).
comp_tit: At the time of each election (first round)
el_flag: The net inflow of voters in 2024 ({saldo}) was larger than the margin of victory ({margem} votes). It is a fact worth context — it does not mean those voters decided the election: the ballot is secret and there is no record of how they voted.
rank_t_bn: Largest share of blank + null votes in the mayoral election (2024 first round), over turnout. A factual figure — it may reflect protest, indecision or a typing error at the machine; it does not indicate fraud.
cr_tit: Legal criteria for an electorate review
cr_1: Transfers in the year +10% vs. the previous year
cr_sim: Meets all 3 criteria. Even so, any review is at the TSE's discretion — this is neither a review nor an indication of irregularity.
cr_nao: Does not meet the 3 cumulative criteria.
cr_fonte: TSE Resolution 23,659/2021, art. 105 — the three are cumulative. Criterion 1 compares 2025 vs. 2024 transfers.
city_frase: There are {per100} registered voters (TSE, {data}) for every 100 estimated residents (IBGE, {ano}).
city_aviso16: A 16+ ratio above 100% is expected here: the denominator counts only those old enough to vote. The total ratio ({razao}) is the more solid reference.
city_ctx_uf: Regional context: it is also atypical for {uf} itself (above {lim}, the state's statistical threshold). A secondary reading — the pattern is strongly regional.
city_tweet: In {local}: {per100} registered voters (TSE) for every 100 estimated residents (IBGE). A high ratio is common and does not indicate irregularity — see the official context:
bdg_80_t: Electorate above 80% of the population — only the 3rd of the THREE cumulative criteria in TSE Resolution 23,659/2021 (art. 105). On its own it does NOT trigger an electorate review.
bdg_atip_t: National statistical outlier: above {lim} (Tukey Q3+1.5·IQR across Brazil)
ins_1: In {local} there are {pct} voters relative to the estimated population.
ins_2: {n} municipalities have more registered voters than estimated residents.
ins_3: {local} had the largest net inflow: {saldo} voters through transfers in {ano}.
ins_4: The oldest electorate is in {local}: {pct} are aged 70+.
ins_5: In {uf}, {pct} of municipalities have more voters than residents.
font_extraido: Extracted on {data} — URL, date and SHA-256 hash of each file in manifest/provenance.json.
metanota_metodo: Each municipality's share of people aged 16+ comes from the 2022 Census (table 9514). Since the annual estimates (6579) carry no age breakdown and do not cover 2022, we apply that 2022 Census share to the year's estimate to obtain the ESTIMATED 16+ population. It is an extrapolation: it assumes the municipality's age structure stayed the same as in 2022.
nota_neutra: Differences may stem from electoral domicile (≠ civil residence), migration with the registration kept, and the fact that the IBGE population is an estimate. A high ratio does not, in itself, indicate any irregularity.
nota_contas: Figures declared in the 2024 electoral accounts (candidates). They are what each campaign reported to the TSE — they do not reflect any judgment of the accounts and do not, in themselves, indicate irregularity. Spending more does not mean buying votes: the ballot is secret.
met_li_gastos: Campaign spending and fundraising — TSE, “Electoral accounts — candidates”, 2024 municipal election (revenues and contracted expenses).
met_li_orcamento: Municipal public budget — National Treasury/SICONFI, 2024 Annual Accounts Declaration (revenue and spending by function: health, education, security).
met_li_votos: Valid, blank and null votes — TSE, “Detailed vote by municipality and zone”, mayor, 2024 first round.
met_aviso_anos: ⚠️ The three datasets are from different years (most recent electorate, population estimate, and the 2022 Census). They are the best available, but the comparison is not from the same moment.
met_li_razao16: 16+ ratio = voters ÷ estimated voting-age population. Since the annual estimate carries no age breakdown, we apply the 2022 Census 16+ share to the year's estimate. It is an extrapolation (it assumes the municipality's age structure stayed the same as in 2022).
met_li_outlier: Statistical outlier = ratio above Q3 + 1.5·IQR (Tukey's rule), computed across Brazil; the per-state reading appears in the detail.
met_h3_80: The 80% marker and the electorate review (what the law says)
met_p_80a: Under TSE Resolution 23,659/2021, art. 105, an ex officio electorate review depends on three CUMULATIVE criteria (all together), and even then it is discretionary:
met_ol_1: transfers in the year ≥ 10% above the previous year;
met_ol_2: electorate greater than twice the population aged 10–15 plus those aged 70+;
met_ol_3: electorate greater than 80% of the population projected by the IBGE.
met_p_80b: We show only the 3rd criterion (>80%) as a marker — on its own it does NOT trigger a review. The TSE itself states that “the disproportion between the number of voters and the population does not, in itself, warrant a review”. That is why ~58% of municipalities exceed 80%, and that, in isolation, means nothing irregular.
met_li_lim1: The IBGE population is an estimate, not an exact count.
met_li_lim2: The 16+ ratio exceeds 100% by construction across much of the country (smaller divisor) — do not read it as an isolated anomaly.
met_li_lim3: Voters abroad (≈917 thousand) are excluded (no IBGE population).
met_li_lim4: The map's boundaries are simplified — a point near a border may fall into the neighbor.
met_li_lim5: A high ratio is common and overwhelmingly legitimate (electoral domicile, migration). This flags where to look, not where there is an irregularity.
met_li_lim6: Campaign spending figures are declared in the 2024 electoral accounts — they do not reflect the TSE's judgment of those accounts and do not, in themselves, indicate irregularity. Spending more does not mean buying votes: the ballot is secret. We use contracted expenses (total declared spending) and revenues (fundraising); “paid expenses” and donors are not included.
met_li_lim7: Public budget (SICONFI) is the city government's money, not campaign money. These are committed expenses and realized revenues declared by the municipality itself; coverage depends on each one's submission and the figures do not reflect any judgment of the accounts. Public security is mostly a state-level function — many municipalities spend little or nothing.
met_repro: Everything is reproducible from the official raw data; each file has a URL, date and hash in manifest/provenance.json.
foot_licenca: Code under the MIT license · derived data under CC-BY 4.0 · original sources: TSE (CC-BY) and IBGE. Every figure is reproducible from the raw files (manifest/provenance.json). Population is always estimated.
orc_tab_t: Highest public spending COMMITTED on Health per capita (city budget ÷ estimated population). Official SICONFI/Treasury data; a declared figure, not a judgment of the accounts. In small municipalities the per-capita figure tends to be higher (fixed costs over a smaller population).
nota_orcamento: The CITY GOVERNMENT's public budget as declared to SICONFI/the National Treasury. These are committed expenses and realized revenues reported by the municipality itself — coverage depends on each city's submission and the figures do not reflect any judgment of the accounts. Do not confuse with campaign spending (TSE): this is public money. Public security is mostly a state-level function — many municipalities spend little or nothing on it.
load_err: Failed to load the data. Run the pipeline (python -m eleitoral.build) to generate docs/data/brasil.json.
geo_indisp: Geolocation is unavailable in this browser.
geo_nao_id: Couldn't identify your municipality in Brazil.
